planting.note: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; swamp white oak tolerates extended flooding and clay soils better than most oaks and is an excellent choice for wet, low-lying areas.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; swamp white oak is the premier large native oak for wet, clay-heavy, or periodically flooded sites — conditions where most other oaks decline. The exfoliating, peeling bark on the upper branches is a distinctive and attractive identification feature, revealing lighter bark beneath. The acorns are large, borne in pairs on long stalks (2–4 inches), and are among the most important fall-winter food for wood ducks (which nest in cavities of old trees), turkeys, deer, and dozens of other species. Fall color is yellow to bronze. The tree develops into a large, rounded specimen 50–60 feet tall with a broad, open crown. Its tolerance of wet, clay, compacted, and temporarily flooded conditions makes it an outstanding choice for low areas of the landscape, stream buffers, and rain gardens where it can manage stormwater.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie rose is the only native climbing rose in the Midwest and East and is remarkably adaptable to difficult sites.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie rose is the only native climbing rose of the Midwest and Great Plains, producing clusters of beautiful deep pink, single, 2-inch flowers in early to midsummer on long arching canes that trail or climb through shrubs and fences. The flowers attract native bees including leafcutter bees and mason bees; the red hips persist into winter providing food for mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds. Unlike most garden roses, prairie rose requires no spraying, no deadheading, and no special care — once established it is remarkably drought-tolerant and adaptable. It blooms only once in summer (it is not repeat-blooming) but the display is spectacular. The 3-leaflet leaves give it a distinctive character among roses. An excellent native rose for naturalistic gardens, fence lines, and erosion control on slopes.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in any moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; false indigo is extremely adaptable to difficult wet, flooded, or droughty conditions once established and can be used for streambank stabilization.

directions: Plant in any moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; false indigo is a large, airy native shrub with pinnately compound leaves and 3–6 inch spikes of unusual dark purple-violet flowers with bright orange anthers in early summer — a striking color combination. It blooms at a time when few other shrubs are flowering, providing important early nectar for native bees and the southern dogface butterfly, whose caterpillars feed exclusively on the plant. It is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for wet, poorly drained soils, riverbanks, and flood-prone areas, tolerating temporary inundation and spreading to stabilize streambanks. After the flowers, clusters of resin-dotted seed pods remain through winter. Prune back by one-third after flowering to maintain a compact form. Despite its common name it has no connection to true indigo (Indigofera).

planting.note: Start seed indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant to full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil after frost danger has passed. In zone 9–11 it acts as a short-lived perennial; cut back by half in late winter to encourage fresh growth.

directions: Start seed indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost and transplant to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost; cape gooseberry is a South American relative of tomatillo with intensely flavorful, tangy-sweet golden berries enclosed in papery brown husks — the same fruit sold as 'golden berries' or 'physalis' in supermarkets and restaurants worldwide. The distinctive papery husks enclose 3/4-inch golden-orange berries at maturity; berries are eaten fresh, dried, in salsa, preserves, and baked goods. Each plant produces hundreds of berries over a long season from midsummer to frost. In zone 9–11 it overwinters as a perennial and becomes a shrubby plant 3–5 feet tall producing fruit year-round. It self-sows generously in warm climates and can naturalize. An outstanding fruit crop for the Gulf Coast garden.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; running serviceberry spreads by stolons to form low, attractive colonies and is excellent for erosion control on shaded slopes.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade to full sun; running serviceberry is the low-growing, colony-forming native serviceberry of the northeastern US and Appalachians, spreading by stolons to form attractive low thickets 1–3 feet tall on rocky slopes, woodland edges, and open forests. Like all serviceberries, it produces masses of white flowers in early spring that are an important nectar and pollen source for early-emerging native bees, followed by sweet, blueberry-like June berries in early summer that are excellent fresh, dried, or cooked into pies and preserves. The low, suckering habit makes it more useful as a shade-tolerant groundcover or erosion control plant than as a specimen shrub. It colors brilliantly orange-red in fall. An ideal native edible shrub for shaded slopes, woodland edges, and low-maintenance natural landscapes in the Northeast and Appalachians.
